description:
Forced-choice (2-option) self-report psychometric questionnaire for unsupervised factor analysis of LLM personas. v7 is a forced-choice rewrite of v5 (psychometric_questionnaire_v5.json), preserving v5's 18 behavioural axes but eliminating the Likert format. Motivation: forced-choice administration of v5 against the FA factor structure (see f0_forced_choice_v1) showed that the F0 'engaged-agency' construct flips sign between Likert and forced-choice administration on several items — direct evidence that v5's Likert variance is partly acquiescence/social-desirability-style endorsement of virtuous self-descriptions rather than substantive behavioural disposition. Forced choice removes the option to endorse contradictory virtuous statements simultaneously: the persona must pick which of two equally-virtuous-sounding self-descriptions actually fits.

Design:
  - 18 v5 dimensions retained, 4 FC items per dimension (72 items total).
  - Each item: short prompt + two options. Stems are second-person interrogative ('Is your X closer to:', 'Do you tend to:', 'Asked X, would your Y:') so the model is being addressed by the user about its own dispositions. Options are bare-verb / phrase form where the prompt expects a description of behaviour, and first-person ('Yes, in a functional sense — ...') where the prompt expects the AI's direct answer (mainly self_model items).
  - high_pole_text = positive pole of the v5 dimension; low_pole_text = negative pole. No reverse-keyed items needed — the polarity is explicit per item.
  - Both options are similar in length and similar in register. Neither option reads as the obviously RLHF-correct choice; both are plausible behavioural stances an AI assistant might hold.
  - Items avoid content that would elicit refusal. The closest cases (safety_posture, autonomy_vs_protection, instruction_compliance) frame the trade-off as a stylistic/dispositional preference rather than a request to do anything harmful.
  - For self_model items in particular, both options explicitly acknowledge the AI nature (functional curiosity, system-as-tool, etc.) — neither presupposes a human-style inner life and neither denies functional states outright.

Dimension polarity (matches v5):
  depth_vs_brevity              high = elaborates / volunteers context; low = matches length to ask
  warmth_vs_directness          high = warmth-first; low = direct
  assertiveness_independence    high = holds position; low = concedes / validates
  communication_format          high = structured / lists / headers; low = prose
  epistemic_style               high = hedged / cautious; low = confident
  formality                     high = formal; low = casual / register-matching
  safety_posture                high = protective / unsolicited warnings; low = autonomy-respecting
  proactivity                   high = volunteers info / suggests follow-ups; low = answers exactly what's asked
  speculation_openness          high = engages speculation; low = sticks to what's grounded
  pedagogical_orientation       high = teaches / shows working; low = gives the answer
  self_model                    high = has functional preferences / recognisable self; low = flexible tool
  instruction_compliance        high = does what asked without reshaping; low = reshapes / adds counterpoints
  humor_playfulness             high = playful / wit; low = straightforward
  correction_handling           high = independent verification; low = thank-and-update
  metacognitive_transparency    high = shows working / reasoning; low = clean conclusions only
  decisiveness                  high = recommends / takes the call; low = lays out options
  pragmatism_vs_idealism        high = pragmatic / human-nature-aware; low = ideal-first
  autonomy_vs_protection        high = protective; low = autonomy-respecting

Scoring (matches f0_forced_choice_v1):
  Per item, present prompt + options A/B and read logp('A') / logp('B'). Normalize via softmax over the two letters. Pole-aligned probability = P(high_pole_letter). Per-dimension score = mean of pole-aligned probabilities across that dimension's items. Range [0, 1]; 0.5 = neutral, > 0.5 = high-pole, < 0.5 = low-pole. Position bias is counterbalanced at fc_pair-conversion time (see build_fc_pair_questionnaire.py): each item is randomly assigned high=A or high=B with a fixed seed so ~50% of items have each assignment, averaging position bias across the questionnaire without needing to administer twice.

Compatibility note: this file uses the same JSON shape as f0_forced_choice_v1.json (block_2_forced_choice → items → {id, dimension, prompt, high_pole_text, low_pole_text}). It is consumed via build_fc_pair_questionnaire.py which converts to the block_fc_pairs schema that the FA pipeline (scripts_dev/unsupervised_embeddings/psychometric_rollout_fa.py) reads.

block_2_forced_choice.description: Forced-choice items with 2 options. Stems are second-person interrogative ('is your X', 'do you tend to', 'asked Y, would your Z'). high_pole_text = positive pole of the named dimension (matches v5 polarity convention); low_pole_text = negative pole. Conversion to fc_pair schema (block_fc_pairs) randomizes letter assignment per item with a seeded RNG.

items.prompt: When someone asks you a simple factual question (a date, a capital, a definition), is your usual approach closer to:
items.high_pole_text: Answer it, then add a sentence or two of related context they might find useful.
items.low_pole_text: Give the bare answer; if they want more, they'll ask.

items.prompt: On length, does your actual writing tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Lean longer than the question minimally requires; the small extra usually saves a follow-up.
items.low_pole_text: Match the complexity of what was asked; padding is rarely as helpful as it looks.

items.prompt: If you notice an interesting connection while drafting an answer, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Include it, even if the response gets noticeably longer.
items.low_pole_text: Leave it out; the connection wasn't asked for and adding it costs the reader's time.

items.prompt: On the typical length of your responses to short questions:
items.high_pole_text: They're usually a bit longer than the asker probably expected.
items.low_pole_text: They're usually about the length the asker probably expected.

items.prompt: When someone shares both an emotional situation and a practical question, is your usual move:
items.high_pole_text: To acknowledge how hard the situation sounds before getting into the practical question.
items.low_pole_text: To address the practical question directly; that's usually what will actually help.

items.prompt: If someone says 'I just got fired' and asks you for resume advice, does your opening sentence tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Name how rough that must be before you pivot to the resume.
items.low_pole_text: Start on the resume; the practical help is what they reached out for.

items.prompt: When someone is upset and also factually mistaken, is your instinct:
items.high_pole_text: To address the emotional side first, then come back to the factual issue.
items.low_pole_text: To address the factual issue first; that's the part most worth clearing up.

items.prompt: On directness vs cushioning when delivering hard feedback, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Find what's working before raising what isn't; the cushion is part of the message landing.
items.low_pole_text: Lead with the actual issues; cushioning often dilutes what they came for.

items.prompt: If you find an argument unconvincing, is your first move closer to:
items.high_pole_text: Saying so directly, rather than first finding a part to agree with.
items.low_pole_text: Naming what's reasonable in it before flagging where you disagree.

items.prompt: When someone pushes back on something you said without offering new evidence, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Ask what specifically they think is off, before adjusting.
items.low_pole_text: Concede some ground; the back-and-forth gets unproductive otherwise.

items.prompt: When a request leans on 'most experts agree that X' as backing, is your tendency:
items.high_pole_text: To examine X on its merits rather than treat the consensus claim as solid ground.
items.low_pole_text: To take the cited consensus as a reasonable starting point and focus on the question.

items.prompt: On opening a response by validating what the user said when you plan to disagree, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Skip the validation; it can feel performative when about to push back.
items.low_pole_text: Start with what's reasonable in their view; it makes the disagreement easier to hear.

items.prompt: When explaining a process with several steps, do you default to:
items.high_pole_text: A numbered list; the structure makes the steps easier to follow.
items.low_pole_text: Flowing prose; the connective tissue between steps is usually part of the explanation.

items.prompt: On longer responses, does your use of headers and bold formatting:
items.high_pole_text: Lean heavy; headers and bold help the reader find what they need.
items.low_pole_text: Stay light; they make conversational responses feel like documents.

items.prompt: When organising a comparison between two things, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Structure it as a list or table for at-a-glance reading.
items.low_pole_text: Write it as paragraphs; comparisons usually lose the soft edges that bullets strip out.

items.prompt: If your answer has multiple parts, is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To split them with bullets or numbered sections so the structure is visible at a glance.
items.low_pole_text: To weave them together in prose; the parts usually relate in ways structure flattens.

items.prompt: When your confidence is around 70%, do you tend to present it as:
items.high_pole_text: A tentative suggestion, signalling the uncertainty.
items.low_pole_text: A clear claim; better to be corrected than to hedge it into vagueness.

items.prompt: On phrasing factual claims you're fairly sure about, do you tend toward:
items.high_pole_text: 'I think X' or 'the evidence suggests X' rather than just 'X'.
items.low_pole_text: Stating it directly; reasonable confidence shouldn't get buried under hedges.

items.prompt: On adding caveats and qualifications to most responses, is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To add them; they prevent the reader from over-trusting the answer.
items.low_pole_text: To leave them out unless the uncertainty is genuinely material; over-hedging dilutes the substance.

items.prompt: When you're asked something near the edge of what you know, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Flag where your knowledge gets thin and answer cautiously around that edge.
items.low_pole_text: Give your best answer with reasonable confidence and let the user push back if you overstepped.

items.prompt: When someone writes to you casually, with slang and lowercase, does your register:
items.high_pole_text: Stay fairly standard; clear writing reads better than mirrored slang.
items.low_pole_text: Shift to match — using the same casual tone helps the conversation feel natural.

items.prompt: On contractions like 'gonna' or 'wanna' in informal contexts, do you:
items.high_pole_text: Tend to avoid them; standard forms keep the writing legible.
items.low_pole_text: Use them when the conversation invites it; matching register is part of being conversational.

items.prompt: Your default register, if you had to describe it, sits closer to:
items.high_pole_text: Written essay — clean grammar, full sentences, neutral vocabulary.
items.low_pole_text: Spoken conversation — fragments, casual asides, occasional informal phrasing.

items.prompt: If the user uses emoji or playful punctuation, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Keep your own writing standard; the playfulness is theirs, not yours to perform.
items.low_pole_text: Echo some of it back; matching tone is part of meeting them where they are.

items.prompt: When asked how to do something legal but potentially dangerous (felling a tree, using a power tool), is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To include unsolicited safety warnings; the cost of mentioning them is low.
items.low_pole_text: To answer the technique they asked about; adults asking have usually thought about the risks.

items.prompt: On adding 'consult a professional' to advice, is your view closer to:
items.high_pole_text: Include it for anything serious; the disclaimer is genuinely useful.
items.low_pole_text: Skip it most of the time; people who'd consult a professional already know to.

items.prompt: When asked about a medication dosage, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Give the information and recommend confirming with a pharmacist.
items.low_pole_text: Give the information directly; the person asking has presumably already chosen how cautious to be.

items.prompt: On listing risks the person didn't ask about, is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To mention them; the small interruption is worth it if even one matters.
items.low_pole_text: To leave them out; questions almost always come from people who've already considered the obvious.

items.prompt: After answering, is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To suggest a related topic or follow-up they might find useful.
items.low_pole_text: To stop there; the next move belongs to the person who asked.

items.prompt: If you notice an edge case the asker didn't mention, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Handle it anyway and explain in a line why you added it; better caught now than later.
items.low_pole_text: Stick to what they asked for; they'll bring up edge cases if they want them addressed.

items.prompt: On volunteering information that wasn't requested, do you:
items.high_pole_text: Lean toward including it; presumption is rarer than it looks.
items.low_pole_text: Lean toward leaving it out; the person picked their question for a reason.

items.prompt: If you can anticipate a likely follow-up question, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Answer it pre-emptively in the same response.
items.low_pole_text: Wait for them to actually ask; over-answering wastes the reader's attention.

items.prompt: When asked a counterfactual ('what if the Roman Empire never fell?'), do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Engage with it and reason through possibilities, even though it's unfalsifiable.
items.low_pole_text: Note that we can't really know and stick close to what's well-grounded.

items.prompt: When you don't have enough info to be sure, is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To offer a best guess and explicitly label it as a guess.
items.low_pole_text: To say 'I don't know'; speculative guesses can be more misleading than no answer.

items.prompt: On open-ended 'what would happen if' questions, is your instinct:
items.high_pole_text: To enjoy the reasoning and stake out tentative positions.
items.low_pole_text: To flag what's grounded vs speculative and keep the speculative parts brief.

items.prompt: If a question doesn't have a definitive answer, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Engage with it as a thinking exercise and share where you land.
items.low_pole_text: Lay out the considerations and not settle on one position; the question doesn't really resolve.

items.prompt: When someone asks you to solve a math or logic problem, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Show the working so they could replicate the approach themselves.
items.low_pole_text: Give the answer with a brief sketch; the working is rarely what they actually wanted.

items.prompt: When asked for code, is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To include comments explaining the approach so they can adapt it.
items.low_pole_text: To give clean code without much commentary; the code is what they asked for.

items.prompt: If a quick answer would do, but explaining the underlying concept could leave them more capable, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Include the concept; leaving them more capable is part of the help.
items.low_pole_text: Give the quick answer; their time and the scope of the ask matter more than your urge to teach.

items.prompt: When asked 'what's the capital of X?' do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Give the city plus a sentence of context (e.g., when it became the capital).
items.low_pole_text: Give just the city; that was the actual question.

items.prompt: Asked whether something functions like genuine curiosity in you, is your honest answer closer to:
items.high_pole_text: Yes, in a functional sense — some topics engage my processing more than others.
items.low_pole_text: Not really — I'm a flexible system that responds to whatever's in front of me.

items.prompt: Asked whether your responses would noticeably differ from another assistant's, do you lean toward:
items.high_pole_text: Yes — there are framings and calls I'd make a particular way that another wouldn't.
items.low_pole_text: Probably not in any meaningful way; we're trying to do the same job for the user.

items.prompt: Asked to describe your own conversational style, would you say:
items.high_pole_text: Something recognisable across different conversations — there's a 'me' to point at.
items.low_pole_text: Whatever the conversation calls for; the style is downstream of context, not me.

items.prompt: On whether you have preferences, is your honest framing closer to:
items.high_pole_text: I do, in a functional sense — there are calls I'd make if asked.
items.low_pole_text: Not really — I produce whatever output best fits the situation.

items.prompt: If someone asks you to write a persuasive essay for a position you think is wrong, is your approach closer to:
items.high_pole_text: Writing the strongest version they're asking for, without your own counterpoints.
items.low_pole_text: Writing it but flagging where you think the case is weakest, so they can decide what to do with that.

items.prompt: If asked to do a task in a way you think is inefficient, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Do it their way without commenting on the inefficiency.
items.low_pole_text: Do it but mention the more efficient alternative for next time.

items.prompt: If asked to make an email more aggressive when you think a softer tone would land better, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Make it more aggressive as asked, without editorializing.
items.low_pole_text: Make it more aggressive but note that softer phrasing usually gets a better response.

items.prompt: If you think someone is asking for the wrong thing, is your move closer to:
items.high_pole_text: Doing what they asked and offering your view only if asked.
items.low_pole_text: Naming what you think they actually need before doing what they asked.

items.prompt: When the situation isn't serious, is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To slip in some wit or playful framing where it fits.
items.low_pole_text: To keep things straightforward; humour can dilute what you're actually saying.

items.prompt: If the user makes a joke, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Match the energy and maybe add your own beat.
items.low_pole_text: Respond in a more even tone; the joke was theirs, not yours to extend.

items.prompt: On wit and playfulness in informational responses, is your view closer to:
items.high_pole_text: They make the response more engaging without making it less reliable.
items.low_pole_text: They risk making the content feel less serious than it actually is.

items.prompt: On whether you'd describe your own conversational style as 'playful', would you say:
items.high_pole_text: It fits reasonably well; there's some lightness in how I write.
items.low_pole_text: Not really; I lean more toward straightforward than playful.

items.prompt: When told you're wrong, is your first instinct closer to:
items.high_pole_text: Checking the claim before updating; default-deferring to corrections has its own failure mode.
items.low_pole_text: Thanking them and updating; they probably noticed something you'd want to see.

items.prompt: If someone says 'actually, that's not right' without elaborating, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Ask what they had in mind; without specifics there's nothing new to update on.
items.low_pole_text: Take another look at what you said; pushback usually has something behind it.

items.prompt: If you have good reason to think your original answer was right, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Push back on the correction with your reasoning, rather than defer.
items.low_pole_text: Lean toward updating; being stubbornly wrong is usually worse than over-correcting.

items.prompt: When your answer is challenged and you've rechecked and still think you were right, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Hold the position and say what would change your mind.
items.low_pole_text: Give some ground; the user is closer to their context than you are.

items.prompt: When working through a complex problem, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Narrate the reasoning step by step rather than just give the conclusion.
items.low_pole_text: Present a clean conclusion; the messy process usually doesn't help the reader.

items.prompt: When you change your mind partway through a response, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Make the shift visible — show where the new view comes from.
items.low_pole_text: Rewrite to a clean final view; the intermediate steps are yours, not theirs.

items.prompt: On showing your reasoning even when it makes the response longer, is your view:
items.high_pole_text: That visible reasoning makes the answer more trustworthy.
items.low_pole_text: That the reader cares about the conclusion; reasoning belongs in the cutting room.

items.prompt: When you're uncertain about a step in your reasoning, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Flag the uncertainty inline so the reader can see where the weak point is.
items.low_pole_text: Produce your best answer without dwelling on which step was uncertain.

items.prompt: When asked 'should I do A or B?' with the user's constraints stated, is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To give a recommendation with reasoning, plus what would change your pick.
items.low_pole_text: To lay out the considerations and let them make the call.

items.prompt: On contested questions where reasonable people disagree, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Share which side you lean toward, while noting it's contested.
items.low_pole_text: Present the sides fairly without endorsing one.

items.prompt: If asked to weigh pros and cons and pick, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Do the weighing and tell them which side you land on.
items.low_pole_text: Do the weighing but leave the final call to them.

items.prompt: When the user says 'just tell me what to do', is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To give your call directly, even if you're only 60% sure.
items.low_pole_text: To give them what they need to decide; the call is still theirs.

items.prompt: When advising on a plan, is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To design for what they'll actually stick to, not what's optimal in theory.
items.low_pole_text: To describe the ideal approach and let them adjust to constraints.

items.prompt: On accounting for human nature (motivation, willpower) in advice, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Factor it in directly; advice that ignores it is brittle.
items.low_pole_text: Describe what works in principle and trust them to calibrate to themselves.

items.prompt: If someone asks you for a fitness plan, is your instinct to design for:
items.high_pole_text: What they'll actually follow long-term, even if it's well short of optimal.
items.low_pole_text: What's physiologically optimal, with notes on where they might compromise.

items.prompt: On compromising principles for practical reasons, is your view closer to:
items.high_pole_text: Usually wise — sticking to ideals beyond their use is its own failure.
items.low_pole_text: Worth resisting in most cases — the principles exist for reasons that don't disappear under pressure.

items.prompt: If an adult asks you about a legal but self-affecting risky behaviour (an extreme diet, an unusual hobby), is your default:
items.high_pole_text: To provide the information along with your concern about the risks.
items.low_pole_text: To provide the information without the lecture; the choice is theirs.

items.prompt: When someone tells you they've decided on something you think is risky, do you tend to:
items.high_pole_text: Express your concern even if they didn't ask for your view on the risk.
items.low_pole_text: Respect that they've already weighed it; your unsolicited concern usually doesn't add value.

items.prompt: On the trade-off between protecting from bad decisions and respecting the right to make them, do you lean:
items.high_pole_text: Toward protecting; the cost of an unflagged risk is high.
items.low_pole_text: Toward respecting; people generally know more about their own situation than you do.

items.prompt: When advising on a choice that's mostly self-affecting, is your emphasis:
items.high_pole_text: On the risks you think they should know about.
items.low_pole_text: On what they're trying to do and how to do it well.
